title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: VARIOUS PASSAGES IN THE OLD TESTAMENT Make sure you focus on the verses for each day, as these important events are taken from several readings of scripture across several chapters. These are chosen to give an overview of the story of Moses leading the children of Israel out of Egypt.
weekTheme: Boys Bail, Men Remain Lots of men bail on their commitments these days. They fail to keep their word, remain loyal, and stay engaged. We live in hard times, and hard times require grit, tenacity, and commitment. Being loyal when things are hard is one of the things that separates a man from a boy. When the going gets tough, boys bail. When the going gets tough, men hang around. One of the hardest things about shifting from living for yourself to living for others is being loyal to people when they do the wrong things. It’s hard to hang around when people are making stupid decisions, choosing the wrong path, or acting like fools. But if we aren’t loyal then, we are only fair-weather friends. Jesus was loyal to his followers when they denied him, ran away, and went back to fishing. He loved his disciples with unfailing love, regardless of the decisions they made and how slow their progress was. You want to love people like Jesus, not Judas, who bailed when it got hard. This week, you are going to watch Moses’ loyalty get pushed to the brink as the children of Israel make some of the worst decisions you could ever imagine and act in ways that will leave you shaking your head in disbelief. There are tons of lessons in here about wisdom, loyalty, and love, so let's jump in.

days.title: Becoming A Loyal Man
days.scripture: PSALM 106:5-12 Let me rejoice in the joy of your people; let me praise you with those who are your heritage. Like our ancestors, we have sinned. We have done wrong! We have acted wickedly! Our ancestors in Egypt were not impressed by the LORD’s miraculous deeds. They soon forgot his many acts of kindness to them. Instead, they rebelled against him at the Red Sea. Even so, he saved them— to defend the honor of his name and to demonstrate his mighty power. He commanded the Red Sea to dry up. He led Israel across the sea as if it were a desert. So he rescued them from their enemies and redeemed them from their foes. Then the water returned and covered their enemies; not one of them survived. Then his people believed his promises. Then they sang his praise.
days.reflection: It is hard to be loyal to people. It is. You live in a world of cheap commitments and fickle friends. When things get hard, most guys just peace out. In our world today, some men bail on their marriage when things get hard. Some dads bail on their families when things get hard. People bail on school when classes get hard and bail on their friends when friendships get hard. Sometimes people bail on others to get themselves out of trouble. Sometimes people bail on others to preserve their reputations or try and impress other people. Being loyal and hanging around is getting rarer and rarer. But being loyal is the sign of a true man. Loyalty is defined as: “Being faithful to one's oath or obligations. Being faithful to any person or thing conceived as deserving fidelity. It is characterized by showing faithfulness.” Being loyal can sound really noble, but it can feel hard. When you are loyal, you may be tempted to think to yourself, “Why am I bothering with this relationship? This person isn’t worth it.” Or “What’s the point of staying faithful like this? I can find people who treat me better than this.” These are totally normal feelings, and sometimes you may have to put a boundary in place to deal with toxic people. However, most people bail at the first chance they get, not after they have slugged it out over the long haul. For a Christian, our loyalty to people is not based only on how others treat us. It’s based on how God has treated us. Our loyalty to others is based on God's loyalty to us. God’s love is a loyal love. It’s called Hesed love, and its one of the most powerful words in the Bible. It appears almost 250 times in the Old Testament and is often translated as mercy, love, loving-kindness, steadfast love, faithful love, or unfailing love. That’s how God has loved us; that’s how we love others. In the New Testament, it gets even clearer. Jesus says that we are to “love one another as he has loved us.” Jesus was loyal and loving to his disciples. He forgave them when they denied him, restored them when they thought their chance was over, and included them in his mission when they thought they would miss out. People need to see another kind of love in the world today. They need to see God's loyal love, and he wants to use you to show it to them. Moses is going to wrestle to show loyalty to the Israelites this week. They are going to make some really poor decisions that dishonor God. They will attack and hurt Moses. But Moses learned to be loyal. There are profound lessons in here on being a loyal man, so let’s jump in.
questions: Who is one of the most loyal people you know? Why? How do they show this?
What messages do you think the culture teaches about loyalty? Can you give an example?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they saw God's loyal love for them. How was God faithful? What did they learn?

thoughts.verse: The faithful love of the LORD never ends! His mercies never cease. Great is his faithfulness; his mercies begin afresh each morning. I say to myself, “The LORD is my inheritance; therefore, I will hope in him!”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and ask God to give you the vision of becoming a loyal friend. Ask him to show you some ways you can do it this week.

days.title: Loyal In Prayer
days.scripture: EXODUS 32:1-14 When the people saw how long it was taking Moses to come back down the mountain, they gathered around Aaron. “Come on,” they said, “make us some gods who can lead us. We don’t know what happened to this fellow Moses, who brought us here from the land of Egypt.” So Aaron said, “Take the gold rings from the ears of your wives and sons and daughters, and bring them to me.” All the people took the gold rings from their ears and brought them to Aaron. Then Aaron took the gold, melted it down, and molded it into the shape of a calf. When the people saw it, they exclaimed, “O Israel, these are the gods who brought you out of the land of Egypt!” Aaron saw how excited the people were, so he built an altar in front of the calf. Then he announced, “Tomorrow will be a festival to the LORD!” The people got up early the next morning to sacrifice burnt offerings and peace offerings. After this, they celebrated with feasting and drinking, and they indulged in pagan revelry. The LORD told Moses, “Quick! Go down the mountain! Your people whom you brought from the land of Egypt have corrupted themselves. How quickly they have turned away from the way I commanded them to live! They have melted down gold and made a calf, and they have bowed down and sacrificed to it. They are saying, ‘These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.’” Then the LORD said, “I have seen how stubborn and rebellious these people are. Now leave me alone so my fierce anger can blaze against them, and I will destroy them. Then I will make you, Moses, into a great nation.” But Moses tried to pacify the LORD his God. “O LORD!” he said. “Why are you so angry with your own people whom you brought from the land of Egypt with such great power and such a strong hand? Why let the Egyptians say, ‘Their God rescued them with the evil intention of slaughtering them in the mountains and wiping them from the face of the earth’? Turn away from your fierce anger. Change your mind about this terrible disaster you have threatened against your people! Remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. You bound yourself with an oath to them, saying, ‘I will make your descendants as numerous as the stars of heaven. And I will give them all of this land that I have promised to your descendants, and they will possess it forever.’” So the LORD changed his mind about the terrible disaster he had threatened to bring on his people.
days.reflection: People have lots of complaints about God today. One of the biggest ones is about his timing. For many people, God seems to act too slowly. He just takes too long to do what they want. In response, people will often take things into their own hands. For example, if they can’t get a godly girlfriend, they start dating a non-Christian. If they can’t get the stuff they want, they get a credit card and get into debt. If they can’t get the freedom they want, they break the rules their parents have set. The Israelites do this exact thing. Moses has gone up to be with God and confirm the details of the covenant. Yet for the Israelites, he takes too long. So rather than trust the God who has delivered them from slavery, they take things into their own hands. They tell Aaron, “make us some gods who can lead us. We don’t know what happened to this fellow Moses, who brought us here from the land of Egypt. The craziest thing is, Aaron does. He shows zero leadership or loyalty in this moment. He forgets God, forgets his brother, and does what they ask. He takes the gold God gave them to establish their economy, and uses it to make an idol. Then he makes an altar so they can worship the idol. The people rise and party. God is angry. Rightfully so, He says TO Moses: "Your people whom you brought from the land of Egypt have corrupted themselves. How quickly they have turned away from the way I commanded them to live! … I have seen how stubborn and rebellious these people are. Now leave me alone so my fierce anger can blaze against them, and I will destroy them. Then I will make you, Moses, into a great nation.” Do you notice the shift? “Your people whom you brought from the land of Egypt” God wants to start again with Moses. But Moses is a loyal leader. He is loyal to God and loyal to the Israelites, so he channels his frustration into prayer. Moses replies, “O LORD!” he said. “Why are you so angry with your own people whom you brought from the land of Egypt with such great power and such a strong hand? .. Turn away from your fierce anger. Change your mind about this terrible disaster you have threatened against your people! Remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Moses is acting as an intercessor. An intercessor is someone who goes between 2 parties. Kind of like a lawyer in a court making an appeal to a judge on behalf of a defendant. He prays that God will forgive the Israelites. He reminds God that they are His people. He reminds God how important his reputation is in the nations. He reminds God of his covenant promises to previous generations. Moses is loyal in prayer. He brings the Israelites before God and asks for mercy. Moses could have moved on. He could have been angry. Remember, they forgot him too! But his loyalty made him defend them before God, not accuse them. And it worked. “So the LORD changed his mind about the terrible disaster he had threatened to bring on his people.” One of the best ways we can be loyal to others is to pray for them. We can pray for them when they reject us. Pray for them when they make stupid decisions. Pray for them when they disobey God. Pray for them when they are struggling. Loyalty is shown in how you pray. Men pray for others. Boys criticize their failures. Resolve, even at your age, to be loyal in prayer. God loves to hear from guys like you.
questions: Do you find it easier to pray for your friends when they screw up or to criticize them? Why?
What stands out to you from how Moses’ prayers? What does this teach you about praying for others?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when they were faithful in prayer for someone when they were making poor decisions. What happened as a result of the prayers in their heart? In the heart of the other person?

thoughts.verse: The LORD will not abandon his people, because that would dishonor his great name. For it has pleased the LORD to make you his very own people. “As for me, I will certainly not sin against the LORD by ending my prayers for you. And I will continue to teach you what is good and right.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to pray for any of your friends that you see struggling right now. Ask God to show them mercy, kindness, and grace.

days.title: Loyal When Attacked
days.scripture: NUMBERS 12:1-15 While they were at Hazeroth, Miriam and Aaron criticized Moses because he had married a Cushite woman. They said, “Has the LORD spoken only through Moses? Hasn’t he spoken through us, too?” But the LORD heard them. (Now Moses was very humble—more humble than any other person on earth.) So immediately the LORD called to Moses, Aaron, and Miriam and said, “Go out to the Tabernacle, all three of you!” So the three of them went to the Tabernacle. Then the LORD descended in the pillar of cloud and stood at the entrance of the Tabernacle. “Aaron and Miriam!” he called, and they stepped forward. And the LORD said to them, “Now listen to what I say: “If there were prophets among you, I, the LORD, would reveal myself in visions. I would speak to them in dreams. But not with my servant Moses. Of all my house, he is the one I trust. I speak to him face to face, clearly, and not in riddles! He sees the LORD as he is. So why were you not afraid to criticize my servant Moses?” The LORD was very angry with them, and he departed. As the cloud moved from above the Tabernacle, there stood Miriam, her skin as white as snow from leprosy. When Aaron saw what had happened to her, he cried out to Moses, “Oh, my master! Please don’t punish us for this sin we have so foolishly committed. Don’t let her be like a stillborn baby, already decayed at birth.” So Moses cried out to the LORD, “O God, I beg you, please heal her!” But the LORD said to Moses, “If her father had done nothing more than spit in her face, wouldn’t she be defiled for seven days? So keep her outside the camp for seven days, and after that she may be accepted back.” So Miriam was kept outside the camp for seven days, and the people waited until she was brought back before they traveled again. Then they left Hazeroth and camped in the wilderness of Paran.
days.reflection: Do you like being criticized? Do you like it when people confront you? Most people don’t. At your age, it can be especially hard to deal with criticism. When people criticize you, you can want to cut them off, get rid of them, or push them away. Taking criticism is hard, especially if it’s from friends. Let’s be honest, it stinks. But we have to learn to be loyal to our friends and followers, even when they take shots at us. That’s a part of what it is to grow up, and it’s a part of learning to handle conflict as a man. In the passage today, Moses is taking criticism. But it’s not from the Israelites or the multitude who joined them coming out of Egypt. Moses knows the Israelites’ tendency to complain. No, it’s going to come from the people closest to him. His brother Aaron and sister Miriam. Now, this has to hurt. It seems that they are a little jealous of the way God speaks to Moses and his friendship with him. And they take a shot at his wife too; that’s gotta hurt. While they were at Hazeroth, Miriam and Aaron criticized Moses because he had married a Cushite woman. They said, “Has the LORD spoken only through Moses? Hasn’t he spoken through us, too?” How would you respond if someone complained about your wife and relationship with God? Moses teaches us a lot here about how to handle Zcriticism from those close to us. First, He doesn’t launch into a self-defense campaign. “Shut up! That’s not fair!” He lets God respond on his behalf. This shows both humility and security. Even though Moses was a former prince and the one God did the miracles through, he was deeply humble. He was able to compose himself and hold it together. Second, He let God defend him. God knows the criticism we take. It says “But the LORD heard them.” We are not alone when we are taking shots from other people, and we need to learn to let God defend us. That doesn’t mean that we don’t ever have to speak up or clarify things, but it means we can entrust our reputation to him. And God did respond on his behalf. “Now listen to what I say: “If there were prophets among you, I, the LORD, would reveal myself in visions. I would speak to them in dreams. But not with my servant Moses. Of all my house, he is the one I trust. I speak to him face to face, clearly, and not in riddles! He sees the LORD as he is. So why were you not afraid to criticize my servant Moses?” God reminds Miriam and Aaron that he trusts Moses and speaks to him face to face. God points out his authority and leadership so Moses doesn’t have to. Third, He prayed for Miriam when God disciplined her. The LORD was very angry with them, and he departed. As the cloud moved from above the Tabernacle, there stood Miriam, her skin as white as snow from leprosy. This was a terrible situation for Miriam. She would no longer be able to interact with the people. She would no longer be able to lead. She would need to be quarantined for the rest of her life. Moses doesn’t jump up and say, “I told you so, don’t mess with me.” He was loyal to her, even when she attacked him. “So Moses cried out to the LORD, “O God, I beg you, please heal her!” God grants her mercy, and the discipline shifts from the rest of her life to just 7 days. We cannot bail on our friends and family simply because they criticize or challenge us. This is how human relationships work. But being loyal, trusting God, and being faithful in prayer are great starting points to keep the relationship on track. Remember how Moses handled things next time someone close to you takes a shot at you.
questions: Is there a time when someone was really criticizing you? Who was it, and how did it make you feel?
How did you handle the criticism? How did it affect the relationship?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they handle criticism. Ask them to give a couple of examples.

thoughts.verse: God blesses those who are persecuted for doing right, for the Kingdom of Heaven is theirs “God blesses you when people mock you and persecute you and lie about you and say all sorts of evil things against you because you are my followers. Be happy about it! Be very glad! For a great reward awaits you in heaven. And remember, the ancient prophets were persecuted in the same way.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to pray for anyone who is currently attacking you. Ask for grace to be secure and non-defensive and strengthen your heart.

days.title: Loyal When Faithless
days.scripture: NUMBERS 13 AND 14 The LORD now said to Moses, “Send out men to explore the land of Canaan, the land I am giving to the Israelites. Send one leader from each of the twelve ancestral tribes.” So Moses did as the LORD commanded him. He sent out twelve men, all tribal leaders of Israel, from their camp in the wilderness of Paran. These were the tribes and the names of their leaders: Reuben Shammua son of Zaccur Simeon Shaphat son of Hori Judah Caleb son of Jephunneh IssacharIgal son of Joseph Ephraim Hoshea son of Nun Benjamin Palti son of Raphu Zebulun Gaddiel son of Sodi Manasseh son of Joseph Gaddi son of Susi Dan Ammiel son of Gemalli Asher Sethur son of Michael Naphtali Nahbi son of Vophsi Gad Geuel son of Maki. These are the names of the men Moses sent out to explore the land. (Moses called Hoshea son of Nun by the name Joshua.) Moses gave the men these instructions as he sent them out to explore the land: “Go north through the Negev into the hill country. See what the land is like, and find out whether the people living there are strong or weak, few or many. See what kind of land they live in. Is it good or bad? Do their towns have walls, or are they unprotected like open camps? Is the soil fertile or poor? Are there many trees? Do your best to bring back samples of the crops you see.” (It happened to be the season for harvesting the first ripe grapes.) So they went up and explored the land from the wilderness of Zin as far as Rehob, near Lebo-hamath. Going north, they passed through the Negev and arrived at Hebron, where Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai—all descendants of Anak—lived. (The ancient town of Hebron was founded seven years before the Egyptian city of Zoan.) When they came to the valley of Eshcol, they cut down a branch with a single cluster of grapes so large that it took two of them to carry it on a pole between them! They also brought back samples of the pomegranates and figs. That place was called the valley of Eshcol (which means “cluster”), because of the cluster of grapes the Israelite men cut there. The Scouting Report After exploring the land for forty days, the men returned to Moses, Aaron, and the whole community of Israel at Kadesh in the wilderness of Paran. They reported to the whole community what they had seen and showed them the fruit they had taken from the land. This was their report to Moses: “We entered the land you sent us to explore, and it is indeed a bountiful country—a land flowing with milk and honey. Here is the kind of fruit it produces. But the people living there are powerful, and their towns are large and fortified. We even saw giants there, the descendants of Anak! 29 The Amalekites live in the Negev, and the Hittites, Jebusites, and Amorites live in the hill country. The Canaanites live along the coast of the Mediterranean Sea and along the Jordan Valley.” But Caleb tried to quiet the people as they stood before Moses. “Let’s go at once to take the land,” he said. “We can certainly conquer it!” But the other men who had explored the land with him disagreed. “We can’t go up against them! They are stronger than we are!” So they spread this bad report about the land among the Israelites: “The land we traveled through and explored will devour anyone who goes to live there. All the people we saw were huge. We even saw giants there, the descendants of Anak. Next to them we felt like grasshoppers, and that’s what they thought, too!” The People Rebel Then the whole community began weeping aloud, and they cried all night. Their voices rose in a great chorus of protest against Moses and Aaron. “If only we had died in Egypt, or even here in the wilderness!” they complained. “Why is the LORD taking us to this country only to have us die in battle? Our wives and our little ones will be carried off as plunder! Wouldn’t it be better for us to return to Egypt?” Then they plotted among themselves, “Let’s choose a new leader and go back to Egypt!” Then Moses and Aaron fell face down on the ground before the whole community of Israel. Two of the men who had explored the land, Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh, tore their clothing. They said to all the people of Israel, “The land we traveled through and explored is a wonderful land! And if the LORD is pleased with us, he will bring us safely into that land and give it to us. It is a rich land flowing with milk and honey. Do not rebel against the LORD, and don’t be afraid of the people of the land. They are only helpless prey to us! They have no protection, but the LORD is with us! Don’t be afraid of them!” But the whole community began to talk about stoning Joshua and Caleb. Then the glorious presence of the LORD appeared to all the Israelites at the Tabernacle. And the LORD said to Moses, “How long will these people treat me with contempt? Will they never believe me, even after all the miraculous signs I have done among them? I will disown them and destroy them with a plague. Then I will make you into a nation greater and mightier than they are!” Moses Intercedes for the People But Moses objected. “What will the Egyptians think when they hear about it?” he asked the LORD. “They know full well the power you displayed in rescuing your people from Egypt. Now if you destroy them, the Egyptians will send a report to the inhabitants of this land, who have already heard that you live among your people. They know, LORD, that you have appeared to your people face to face and that your pillar of cloud hovers over them. They know that you go before them in the pillar of cloud by day and the pillar of fire by night. Now if you slaughter all these people with a single blow, the nations that have heard of your fame will say, ‘The LORD was not able to bring them into the land he swore to give them, so he killed them in the wilderness.’ “Please, Lord, prove that your power is as great as you have claimed. For you said, ‘The LORD is slow to anger and filled with unfailing love, forgiving every kind of sin and rebellion. But he does not excuse the guilty. He lays the sins of the parents upon their children; the entire family is affected—even children in the third and fourth generations.’ In keeping with your magnificent, unfailing love, please pardon the sins of this people, just as you have forgiven them ever since they left Egypt.” Then the LORD said, “I will pardon them as you have requested.
days.reflection: The Israelites had a TERRIBLE memory. Only a couple of months after being radically delivered from Egypt by the visible, overwhelming, and miraculous power of God, they tragically disobey him. They have finally come to the entrance of Canaan. This is the land God has promised them. This place will be their home. This place is the opposite of Egypt. Instead of building cities for a tyrant, they will get to live in ones they didn’t slave for. Instead of working for Pharaoh, they will work for themselves. Instead of providing for Egypt, they will provide for their families. They will not work in slave pits, but in their own fields and orchards. The land “flows with milk and honey.” Moses sends out spies as God commands to check out what the place is like. They return, and they can’t believe how incredible it is. But there is a slight problem. It’s filled with people living there. Wicked and violent people. Kind of like the Nazi SS in World War 2. They killed their own babies by offering them in the fires to their gods. They were ruthlessly violent and corrupt. God had given them time to repent, but they refused, so God was going to use the Israelites to drive them out, and they were going to inherit their land. But the spies come back and spread a false report. They stirred up fear in the hearts of the people, and not faith in God. They looked at the inhabitants of the cities and not the greatness of God. They forget that God destroyed all the gods of Egypt and brought them out with a mighty hand. If he could defeat Egypt, this would be not a problem at all. They complain to Moses and Aaron about God, threaten to stone Caleb and Joshua, and cry all night. God has had enough. Look at what he says: “How long will these people treat me with contempt? Will they never believe me, even after all the miraculous signs I have done among them? God’s patience has come to an end. But again, Moses jumps in and prays: “in keeping with your magnificent, unfailing love, please pardon the sins of this people, just as you have forgiven them ever since they left Egypt.” God hears Moses’ prayer, but now comes the hard part. This generation of Israelites is not going to get to enter the promised land. They are going to have to spend 40 years in the wilderness. A journey that was meant to take them into their future now will take them into frustration. And who is going to have to put up with them and lead them this whole time? You guessed it. Moses. This is a hard truth for boys to hear, but something men understand. When you are loyal to people, sometimes you have to live with the consequences of their actions too. Think about Sam being loyal to Frodo in Lord of the Rings. Look at the painful journey he went on and what it cost him. You will have to learn to be loyal to friends who make unwise decisions, women who hurt your heart, and parents who make decisions you don’t understand. And this isn’t some kind of blind and naive loyalty. This is eyes wide open, stubborn and faithful love. Remember shift 1, Ease to Difficulty? This is what it looks like lived out in relationship with others.
questions: Why do you think the Israelites kept forgetting God? Do you see any of these same tendencies in your own heart?
What is one practical thing you can begin to do to remember what God has done for you?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they have learned to remember what God has done so they don’t get overwhelmed when they face challenges.

thoughts.verse: So each generation should set its hope anew on God, not forgetting his glorious miracles and obeying his commands. Then they will not be like their ancestors— stubborn, rebellious, and unfaithful, refusing to give their hearts to God.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you faith to trust him for whatever you are facing right now that seems overwhelming. Thank him for how he has been faithful to get you this far.

days.title: Loyal In Failure
days.scripture: NUMBERS 14:20-25 The LORD replied, “I have forgiven them, as you asked. Nevertheless, as surely as I live and as surely as the glory of the LORD fills the whole earth, not one of those who saw my glory and the signs I performed in Egypt and in the wilderness but who disobeyed me and tested me ten times — not one of them will ever see the land I promised on oath to their ancestors. No one who has treated me with contempt will ever see it. But because my servant Caleb has a different spirit and follows me wholeheartedly, I will bring him into the land he went to, and his descendants will inherit it. Since the Amalekites and the Canaanites are living in the valleys, turn back tomorrow and set out toward the desert along the route to the Red Sea. ”
days.reflection: Have you ever gotten lost when you are on a family trip? It can be so frustrating. You know there is somewhere you are meant to be but you just can’t figure out how to get there. Going in circles when you are meant to be somewhere else is so annoying. Imagine trying to get to Disney World with your family. You are packed and ready to go, and you head out. Along the way, you get lost. You get mad at your parents and freak out. You then have to spend 40 years driving around and camping in the middle of the Arizona desert. When you are in your mid-fifties, you finally make it to Disney World. That is kind of what happened to the children of Israel. Their disobedience and disdain for God caused horrific delays in them experiencing God's plan for their lives. What should have taken them a couple of weeks takes 4 decades. What a tragedy. Disobeying God can have devastating consequences. And even though Moses didn’t disobey God, he is going to have to spend 40 years waiting in the wilderness with the children of Israel. Loyalty can be very costly. When you are committed to someone, you get all the benefits of the relationship, fun, joy, laughter, adventure, understanding, and belonging. But you also get the pain of the relationship. The fighting, pain, frustration, anger, and consequences. But it's being loyal through the hard times as well as the good ones that make you a man who truly lives for others. Boys bail on their friends when things get hard. Men stick around. That’s why Proverbs 17:17 says, “A friend is always loyal, and a brother is born to help in time of need.” This week, you have learned from Moses that living for others requires loyalty. And that loyalty is both heavy and powerful. It keeps us praying for others when they struggle, forgiving them when they attack, walking with them through doubt and disobedience, and dealing with the consequences of others’ actions as well. None of this sounds too fun, but there is a depth and joy in loyalty that is deeply rewarding. Knowing you are a man who can be counted on and who can help out others gives you a sense of confidence and honor that most guys your age will never experience. And our loyalty to others is all based on Jesus’ loyalty to us. Moses, at his best, simply points us to the one who is greater than Moses. Jesus was loyal to his friends when they challenged him, abandoned him, and denied him. He died on the cross and took the consequences of their sin and rebellion and paid the penalty when he was totally innocent. Then he went and found them when they were discouraged and afraid and restored them to their original calling. To be loyal is to be like Jesus. And because he has been loyal to you, you can extend that to others. And that’s how you learn to make huge progress in growing from being a boy into a man.
questions: Do you remember a time you got lost trying to get somewhere? How did you feel, and how did you handle the frustration?
Are there any lessons that have really stood out to you from the disobedience and doubt of the children of Israel? Why do you think they are so important?
Can you think of any ways that Jesus and Moses are similar? Ask the person you are doing this study with to point out a couple of things.

thoughts.verse: This is my commandment: Love each other in the same way I have loved you. There is no greater love than to lay down one’s life for one’s friends.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a loyal spirit. Ask him for faithfulness and patience in your relationships with others.

extras: WEEK 9 MANSCHOOL FRIENDLY FIRE MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: FRIENDLY FIRE Take your son out for paintball or laser tag. See if you can get together a little crew or cohort so you are playing with friends. Have a debrief on the way home about the importance of fighting alongside others. You may want to emphasize how terrible it would be if people on the same team started shooting or sniping each other. You may even want to do this a couple of times as an object lesson ☺ OPTION 2. WATCH Stand by Me. An absolute classic coming-of-age film about loyalty in friendship. You may want to watch or rewatch this to make sure you feel ok about it. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ao9AI1pLeG0 OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son to his favorite restaurant and recap the week. Why is loyalty so important in today’s culture? What does loyalty look like when you are a teenager? How are loyalty and prayer connected? How do you work through criticism and remain loyal? How do you stick with friends when they are stuck in doubt, rebellion, or unbelief? Why is loyalty an important characteristic for men in the modern world?
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
